narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 42% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 39, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income bracket in the lower-middle range, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on those most predictive of bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance that often correlates with bar outcomes. Counterbalancing this, your LSAT score stood out positively as one of the next most influential factors; at 39, it's higher than the 37.27 average among bar passers, which pulled the prediction toward success and helped keep your failure risk from being even higher. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 also contributed favorably in a similar way, though less strongly, as it's close to but slightly below the 3.24 average for those who passed, offering some reassurance of your foundational abilities. While your lower-middle family income is less common among bar passers—where middle and upper-middle brackets dominate—and your full-time status aligns with most successful students, these played smaller roles compared to your grades and test scores. Overall, your Year 3 performance tipped the scales the most, overriding the boosts from your stronger LSAT and solid GPA. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have higher decile rankings across the board and similar or slightly lower LSATs, your profile showed promise in some areas but raised flags in academic consistency. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven risk assessment to ensure we admit those with the highest predicted odds of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Many students in similar situations improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—reach out to our admissions office for personalized advice on building a stronger case next time.
